items.q: What does it mean that RAM is volatile?
items.correct: Its contents are lost when power is removed.
items.misconception: It can explode or be damaged easily.
items.why: Students take 'volatile' from chemistry literally. In computing, volatile means contents don't persist without power - that's why RAM is used for working memory, not storage.

items.correct: The ability of different classes to respond to the same method call in their own way.
items.misconception: Having multiple classes with the same name.
items.random_wrong: Variables that change type at runtime.
items.why: Students confuse polymorphism with naming. Polymorphism means 'many forms' - a single interface (method signature) behaving differently depending on the object's actual class.

items.q: What is the worst-case complexity of bubble sort?
items.misconception: O(n) because it just passes through the list.
items.why: Students count one pass and forget that bubble sort does up to n passes, each comparing up to n elements. The nested loop structure gives n times n = n squared.

items.q: Ziegler-Nichols PID tuning method was published in:
items.why: Correct answer: 1942. “1958” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A thermostat is an example of:
items.why: Correct answer: Negative feedback. “Positive feedback” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Year Ziegler and Nichols published PID tuning method?
items.why: The real figure is 1942 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
